comment.body:
I see some back and forth on things, confusing intraday local dealer hedging flow effects with non-local non-intraday dealer hedging flow effects.   
There is difference between intraday local effects and wider-term net effects, that are derived solely from dealer hedging mechanism. But there are other flows in the market as well.

For example, just like now, some plp can say that "you said suppressive flows in yesterday post!! but now you say 'oh i said supportive opex' so you are right anyway".
Nope... 
I'm talking about supportive march opex since feb, even despite the respected furus are talked about doomsday, flush etc. for this week.
The LOCAL INTRADAY vanna charm flows were suppressive yesterday at a certain range in terms of not letting any rally, not letting the upward move to gain momentum. 
(You have to understand what these flows mean in order to understand this. I link the OD chat in every intraday post. Take your time.)
But the OVERALL positioning is supportive into this OpEx + as I keep saying: HF algos are in mean reversion mode as you can see in the price action clearly. 

You have to learn to think in different tenors paralel. So for example while locally your portfolio gains delta that you neutralize, on a hourly, or daily tenor it still can lose delta that you have to buy back later. (Very much simplified) 
NOTHING is moving in straight linear lines!
This is where most of the retails are lost.

This community is full of high knowledge plp. Please, ask proper questions after you made it sure that you didn't find answers in my posts or in the internet... in order to avoid wasting other's time as a result of simple laziness for thinking. 

And opex has never meant EOM, as many other members pointed that out. March OpEx is March OpEx. If you use the search tools in chat and in the substack site, you will see that I use this term, also when I wrote about the misconceptions about JPM collar (that expires on EOM), I literally differentiated these two (also making it clear and showing you how small effect the collar has currently, it only has local effects) Look back that post!

Life is complicated and getting even more complicated.
Don't make it even more difficult for yourself with unnecessary overthinking of things that shouldn't be, while not thinking on things that actually should be thought through.

comment.body: I think the best way to start to get an understanding of the Greeks is to draw the price. Then the delta underneath, then using the tangent on the delta to draw the gamma you would expect, from the gamma curve you can then draw the speed. It sort of instantly explains where and when you would expect to positive speed etc. a similar approach can be used on other Greeks. It’s a good place to start, I personally am new to most of this so no expert but that was helpful for me. So I could begin to grasp some of the chat.

Last week someone kindly referenced this document as providing a good explanation of dealer hedging mechanics https://squeezemetrics.com/download/The_Implied_Order_Book.pdf
I agree it is quite helpful.  It's entirely consistent with and dovetails nicely into Alma's description delta, gamma, and vanna, and relates that to dealer hedging mechanics. comment.body:
Those are just the % of 1st Std, 2nd std, 3rd std of a normal distribution curve. So if you have certain level of volatility you can work out how far the market should move that day away from its start point and assign a probability to it. If we are at 2nd std deviation down well you are below 95.4% of the possible down expectation and 100% of the possible up expectations. So in effect you are below 97.6% of the possible moves that day. So possibly not a bad place to try a long all other things being equal. But normal distribution don’t describe the real world, the real world has lumpy tails. 

I think these lumpy tails are modelled now in option pricing but in the old days I think some people made a lot of money by placing regular far OTM bets that Black Scholes would price using normal distributions. These people understood NDist was not a perfect approximation of reality

comment.body: Great question. The approach is fairly similar, but the option pricing always also includes market factors that influence the distribution. When there is a lot of supply on a certain point or area of the vol surface that distorts the distribution. Also it would make it by definition impossible to benefit from long or short vol because your market model and your option price model are based on the exact same distribution. What you want to profit from (the edge Alma is referring to) is the mispricing of the real odds, e.g. the market prices a different outcome than the underlying statistical process would have you expect. So in practice what you do is you configure the statistical process (the Wiener process/Brownian motion) to have fatter tails, for example by including random jumps). If I recall correctly Alma covered this in one of her first articles
